mappings.rationale: Directly covers the periodic law, classification of elements, and the long form of the periodic table that is the central topic of this section.
Covers the chemical elements, their properties, history, and classification directly relevant to every subsection of this section.
Covers the systematic classification of elements, their chemical and physical properties, and periodic trends discussed throughout this section.
Covers the principal compounds of elements and the chemical properties that determine element groupings in the periodic table.
Covers the historical development of the periodic law and the discovery and classification of chemical elements discussed in this section.
Directly relevant to stable and radioactive elements, isotopic composition, and the classification of elements as stable or radioactive.
Relevant to the section's coverage of radioactive elements, transuranium elements, and the actinide series.
Relevant to the origin of elements through nucleosynthesis and the synthetic transuranium elements discussed in this section.
Covers the physical and chemical properties of elements, atomic structures, and the basis for periodic classification.
Relevant to the occurrence and relative abundances of elements in the Earth's crust and the natural occurrence of native and combined elements.
Relevant to the abundance and distribution of elements in the Earth's crust discussed in the section's coverage of elemental abundances on Earth.
Relevant to the distribution of elements in the Earth's crust, hydrosphere, and atmosphere discussed in this section.
Relevant to the origin of elements in stars through nucleosynthesis and the abundances of elements in stellar systems.
Relevant to the origin of elements and their relative abundances in the universe, including primordial nucleosynthesis.
Directly relevant to hydrogen and water, their structure, forms, and properties as discussed in the section's first element group.
Covers the nature of chemical elements, metals, nonmetals, and their fundamental properties relevant to the classification scheme in this section.
Relevant to the physical properties of metallic elements, semiconductors, and the electronic structure underlying elemental classification.
Relevant to the technologically significant elements and the physical and chemical properties of metals and nonmetals discussed in this section.
Provides historical context for the early discovery and classification of chemical elements that led to the periodic law.
Relevant to the physical properties and crystal structures of elements, particularly metals and their alloys discussed in this section.
Relevant to the chemistry of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and other elements whose compounds define major branches of chemistry.
Relevant to the distribution and abundance of elements in the hydrosphere discussed in the section's coverage of elemental abundances on Earth.
Relevant to the abundance of elements in the Earth's atmosphere, particularly the noble gases, nitrogen, and oxygen discussed in this section.
Relevant to the special physical properties of certain metallic elements and transition metals discussed in this section.
